It is something to pass forward.

cta.label: BEGIN YOUR JOURNEY
cta.title: Let's Create Something Timeless
cta.subtitle: Every yurt is unique. Share your vision, and we'll bring it to life with traditional craftsmanship and modern expertise.

steps.title: Design & Consultation
steps.desc: Understanding your vision, space requirements, and intended use. Every yurt begins with a conversation about purpose and place.

steps.title: Material Selection
steps.desc: Choosing premium natural materials — sustainable wood from Kazakh forests, hand-pressed felt, and weather-resistant canvas.

steps.title: Frame Construction
steps.desc: Master craftsmen shape and bend each wooden lattice wall by hand, creating the iconic circular structure.

steps.title: Felt Making
steps.desc: Traditional wool pressing techniques passed through generations. Each layer carefully compressed for natural insulation.

steps.title: Assembly & Details
steps.desc: Hand-stitching decorative elements, painting traditional patterns, ensuring every joint meets our exacting standards.

steps.title: Global Installation
steps.desc: Our team travels worldwide to install your yurt, ensuring it stands perfectly in its new home.

about.title: What is Tengri Yurt?
about.text: Tengri Yurt is a handcrafted architectural space rooted in the ancient nomadic traditions of Kazakhstan and reimagined for the modern world. Designed as a circular environment of connection and presence, it brings people together in nature — for retreats, eco-resorts, cultural projects, and transformational gatherings across the globe.

modal.title: A Brief History of the Yurt
modal.intro: The yurt (known as ger in Mongolian) is a portable, circular dwelling made of a flexible wooden lattice frame, covered with felt or skins. It has been the iconic home of nomadic peoples across Central Asia for over 3,000 years.
modal.origins_text: The word "yurt" comes from Turkic roots meaning "homeland," "dwelling," or "place of stay." The earliest evidence dates back to the Bronze Age and Scythian nomads around 600 BC. Turkic nomads popularized the trellis-walled design by the 6th century AD.
modal.history_text: Yurts were perfect for nomadic life — quick to assemble (about 1 hour), lightweight, wind-resistant, insulated against extreme temperatures. During the Mongol Empire, Genghis Khan used large yurts as mobile command centers.
modal.structure_text: Round for stability and airflow. Key parts: foldable lattice walls (kerege), roof poles (uni), central roof wheel (toono/shanyrak), felt layers for waterproofing and insulation.
modal.modern_text: Today yurts inspire glamping, eco-retreats, and modern adaptations worldwide. They symbolize nomadic heritage, sustainability, and connection to nature.
modal.conclusion: The yurt is one of humanity's oldest, smartest portable homes — born on the steppes, built to move with the people, and still thriving today.
